title: Charts, Boards & Stakeholder Models
summary: A curated PMP guide to the exam visuals that turn project data, flow, ownership, risk and stakeholder analysis into decisions.
intro: Use these charts, boards and models as tailored artifacts, not paperwork. The exam usually asks what the visual tells you and what action protects value next.

sections.heading: Why these visuals matter
sections.body: PMBOK 8 is principle- and outcome-based. Charts, boards and matrices are artifacts you tailor to context so the team can see work, understand signals and make timely decisions. Most questions are not asking you to admire the visual - they are asking how to use it to protect value.

blocks.title: Visual -> what it answers -> where it fits

rows.value: Which few causes drive most defects? -> Scope / Quality.
Is the process stable and in control? -> Monitoring & controlling / Quality.
How much work is left this sprint? -> Schedule / monitoring.
How much is done, and did scope change? -> Scope / monitoring.
What are the possible root causes? -> Risk / Scope.
How is work flowing? -> Governance + Scope.
Who does what, and who owns the outcome? -> Resources.
Where should stakeholder engagement effort go? -> Stakeholders.
Which risks get a response first? -> Risk.

blocks.title: Tailoring reminder
blocks.text: Artifacts are chosen, not mandated. Pick the lightest visual that fits the delivery approach and drives a decision; be suspicious of an answer that says to always produce the full matrix.

sections.heading: Pareto chart
sections.body: A Pareto chart is a bar chart of causes ordered from most to least frequent, with a line showing the running cumulative percentage. It is a quality tool used to find which few causes drive most of the problems, so you fix those first. It supports the Scope domain and the Embed Quality principle by focusing prevention where it pays off.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='40' y1='18' x2='40' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='300' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='52' y='52' width='34' height='108' fill='#1F5FA8'/><rect x='100' y='82' width='34' height='78' fill='#1F5FA8'/><rect x='148' y='112' width='34' height='48' fill='#1F5FA8'/><rect x='196' y='134' width='34' height='26' fill='#1F5FA8'/><rect x='244' y='148' width='34' height='12' fill='#1F5FA8'/><polyline points='69,50 117,32 165,23 213,19 261,17' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='69' cy='50' r='3' fill='#B5462F'/><circle cx='117' cy='32' r='3' fill='#B5462F'/><circle cx='165' cy='23' r='3' fill='#B5462F'/><circle cx='213' cy='19' r='3' fill='#B5462F'/><circle cx='261' cy='17' r='3' fill='#B5462F'/><text x='170' y='182' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>causes — most frequent first</text></svg>
figure.caption: Bars: frequency (descending). Line: cumulative %.

groups.label: Bars
lines: Frequency of each cause, tallest on the left, descending to the right.

groups.label: Line
lines: Cumulative percentage climbing left to right, flattening near 100 percent.

groups.label: Decision
lines: Attack the tall left-hand bars first for the biggest quality gain.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Pareto = the 80/20 rule: focus on the 'vital few' causes on the left, not the 'trivial many' on the right. It ranks discrete categories, not a trend over time, and is often paired after a fishbone: fishbone finds candidate causes, Pareto ranks the ones that actually occur.

sections.heading: Control chart
sections.body: A control chart shows measurements over time with a centre line, or process mean, plus upper and lower control limits. It tells you whether a process is stable and 'in control'. This is monitoring and controlling in action: distinguish normal variation from a signal worth investigating.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='40' y1='30' x2='300' y2='30' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='4 4'/><line x1='40' y1='150' x2='300' y2='150' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='4 4'/><line x1='40' y1='90' x2='300' y2='90' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><polyline points='55,96 82,78 109,102 136,74 163,96 190,70 217,100 244,22 271,86' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='55' cy='96' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='82' cy='78' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='109' cy='102' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='136' cy='74' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='163' cy='96' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='190' cy='70' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='217' cy='100' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='244' cy='22' r='4' fill='#B5462F'/><circle cx='271' cy='86' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><text x='304' y='30' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10' text-anchor='end'>UCL</text><text x='304' y='150' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10' text-anchor='end'>LCL</text><text x='304' y='86' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='end'>mean</text></svg>
figure.caption: The red point above the UCL is out of control.

groups.label: Action
lines: Leave it alone - only common-cause variation is present.

groups.label: Pattern
lines: A point beyond UCL or LCL.
A non-random run, such as the rule of seven.

groups.label: Action
lines: Investigate the special cause; assign and remove it.

items.text: Upper / lower control limits - the boundary of expected variation, set from the process, usually +/- 3 sigma.
Customer requirement limits - not the same as control limits; control limits are process-driven.
Seven points in a row on one side of the mean signals a non-random trend, even inside the limits.
Normal, inherent variation; do not react to it.
An assignable, unusual event to investigate.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Out of control = a point beyond UCL/LCL, or the rule of seven. Do not confuse control limits, the process voice, with specification limits, the customer voice. Reacting to common-cause variation, or 'tampering', makes a stable process worse.

sections.heading: Cause-and-effect fishbone
sections.body: A cause-and-effect diagram, also called a fishbone or Ishikawa diagram, is a root-cause tool. The effect, or problem, sits at the head; angled bones group possible causes into categories, with finer sub-causes branching off. It structures brainstorming so you investigate causes by category rather than at random.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 340 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='16' y1='100' x2='266' y2='100' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.6'/><polygon points='266,94 278,100 266,106' fill='currentColor'/><rect x='280' y='84' width='52' height='32' rx='4' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='306' y='104' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Effect</text><line x1='70' y1='44' x2='104' y2='100' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='170' y1='44' x2='204' y2='100' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='70' y1='156' x2='104' y2='100' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='170' y1='156' x2='204' y2='100' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='66' y='38' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>People</text><text x='166' y='38' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Process</text><text x='66' y='170' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Tools</text><text x='166' y='170' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Environment</text></svg>
figure.caption: Causes grouped on bones; the problem (effect) at the head.

rows.value: Machine, Method, Material, Manpower, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment).
People, Process, Tools, Environment.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Fishbone = Ishikawa = cause-and-effect diagram. It explores possible causes; it does not prove them. Pair it with data, such as Pareto or 5 Whys, to confirm.

sections.heading: Burndown chart
sections.body: A burndown chart shows work remaining over a sprint or release. The straight ideal line runs from all the work down to zero; the actual line is the real remaining work each day. It is a Schedule-domain progress artifact tailored to an adaptive approach, giving the team fast, self-managed feedback.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='40' y1='20' x2='40' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='300' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='44' y1='34' x2='296' y2='158' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='5 4'/><polyline points='44,34 90,52 136,86 182,96 228,128 296,158' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='2'/><text x='250' y='52' fill='currentColor' font-size='10'>ideal</text><text x='150' y='80' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='10'>actual</text><text x='170' y='182' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>sprint days</text></svg>
figure.caption: Work remaining vs the ideal line, across the sprint.

groups.label: Ideal line
lines: Straight line from total work down to zero at sprint end.

groups.label: Actual above ideal
lines: More work remaining than planned = behind.

groups.label: Actual below ideal
lines: Less work remaining than planned = ahead.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Burndown shows work remaining, trending down toward zero. It can hide scope changes: a flat line could mean added scope offsetting progress, so use burn-up to tell them apart.

sections.heading: Burn-up chart
sections.body: A burn-up chart plots work completed against a separate line for total scope. Because scope is its own line, you can see scope changes that a burndown would hide, directly supporting the Scope domain's focus on deliverables and realised value.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='40' y1='20' x2='40' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='300' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><polyline points='44,60 170,60 170,42 296,42' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.6' stroke-dasharray='5 4'/><polyline points='44,158 92,140 140,110 188,96 236,70 296,46' stroke='#2F6F5E' stroke-width='2'/><text x='250' y='36' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10'>scope</text><text x='150' y='118' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='10'>completed</text><text x='170' y='182' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>sprint days</text></svg>
figure.caption: Completed work rising toward total scope (which steps up when scope is added).

groups.label: Completed line
lines: Rises over time toward the scope line.

groups.label: Scope line
lines: Steps up when work is added, down when removed.

groups.label: Done when
lines: The completed line meets the scope line.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Burn-up separates progress from scope. A jump in the top line means scope was added, not that the team slowed down. The gap between the two lines is the remaining work.

sections.heading: Kanban board
sections.body: A Kanban board pulls work through columns, typically To Do, In Progress and Done. A work-in-progress limit caps how many items a column may hold, exposing bottlenecks and keeping the team focused on finishing before starting.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 330 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='58' y='20' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>To Do</text><text x='165' y='20' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>In Progress</text><text x='165' y='33' fill='#B5462F' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>WIP limit 2</text><text x='272' y='20' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Done</text><rect x='10' y='40' width='96' height='150' rx='5' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><rect x='117' y='40' width='96' height='150' rx='5' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.3'/><rect x='224' y='40' width='96' height='150' rx='5' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><rect x='18' y='50' width='80' height='24' rx='3' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='18' y='80' width='80' height='24' rx='3' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='18' y='110' width='80' height='24' rx='3' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='125' y='50' width='80' height='24' rx='3' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.3'/><rect x='125' y='80' width='80' height='24' rx='3' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.3'/><rect x='232' y='50' width='80' height='24' rx='3' fill='#E7F1ED' stroke='#2F6F5E' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='232' y='80' width='80' height='24' rx='3' fill='#E7F1ED' stroke='#2F6F5E' stroke-width='1'/></svg>
figure.caption: Cards pulled left to right; the middle column is capped at its WIP limit.

items.title: Visualise the work
lines: Every item is a card in a column.
Columns model your real workflow stages.

items.title: Limit WIP
lines: Lower the limit to surface bottlenecks.

items.title: Pull, don't push
lines: Pull a new card only when capacity frees up.

rows.value: To Do -> In Progress -> Done, or columns that mirror your workflow.
The key lever; a capped column exposes the constraint.
New work starts only when downstream frees up.
Lead time, cycle time and throughput; a cumulative flow diagram shows trends.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Kanban = visualise flow, limit WIP, pull work. A WIP limit is the primary lever; reduce bottlenecks by finishing work, not by starting more.

sections.heading: RACI responsibility assignment matrix
sections.body: A RACI is a responsibility assignment matrix that maps activities to roles and records each role's involvement. Rows are activities or deliverables; columns are roles or people. The matrix clarifies who does the work, who owns the result, who gives input and who is kept informed.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 330 175' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='10' y1='10' x2='320' y2='10' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='10' y1='40' x2='320' y2='40' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='10' y1='74' x2='320' y2='74' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><line x1='10' y1='108' x2='320' y2='108' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><line x1='10' y1='142' x2='320' y2='142' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='10' y1='10' x2='10' y2='142' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='120' y1='10' x2='120' y2='142' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='170' y1='10' x2='170' y2='142' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.6'/><line x1='220' y1='10' x2='220' y2='142' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.6'/><line x1='270' y1='10' x2='270' y2='142' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.6'/><line x1='320' y1='10' x2='320' y2='142' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><text x='145' y='30' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>PM</text><text x='195' y='30' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>Sponsor</text><text x='245' y='30' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Team</text><text x='295' y='30' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>QA</text><text x='16' y='61' fill='currentColor' font-size='10'>Charter</text><text x='16' y='95' fill='currentColor' font-size='10'>Plan</text><text x='16' y='129' fill='currentColor' font-size='10'>Build</text><text x='145' y='61' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>R</text><text x='195' y='61' fill='#B5462F' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>A</text><text x='245' y='61' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>C</text><text x='295' y='61' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>I</text><text x='145' y='95' fill='#B5462F' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>A</text><text x='195' y='95' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>C</text><text x='245' y='95' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>R</text><text x='295' y='95' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>I</text><text x='145' y='129' fill='#B5462F' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>A</text><text x='195' y='129' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>I</text><text x='245' y='129' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>R</text><text x='295' y='129' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>C</text><text x='165' y='162' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>R responsible · A accountable · C consulted · I informed</text></svg>
figure.caption: Roles across the top, activities down the side; one A per row.

items.text: Responsible - does the work. One or more per activity.
Accountable - owns the outcome. Exactly one per activity.
Informed - kept in the loop; one-way communication.

rows.value: Single point of accountability; it cannot be shared.
No owner - a gap to fix.
One role is R on too much - a bottleneck.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Exactly one 'A' per row. Several can be R, C or I, but accountability cannot be shared. RACI is a form of RAM; a RAM can also roll up at a summary level.

sections.heading: Probability-impact matrix
sections.body: A probability-impact matrix scores each risk by probability and impact, giving a risk score that sets priority. It drives qualitative risk analysis: which risks get a response first. Both threats and opportunities live on the matrix.

groups.label: Red - high
lines: Respond first; assign an owner now.

groups.label: Green - low
lines: Accept or add to a watch list.

blocks.title: Scoring
blocks.note: Same math sizes threats and opportunities.

lines: Rank all risks, then respond top-down.
Sum identified risks to size contingency reserve.

rows.value: Escalate, Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept.
Escalate, Exploit, Share, Enhance, Accept.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Probability x Impact sets qualitative priority. The matrix ranks risks; it does not resolve them by itself. New risk -> register it, analyse P and I, then plan the response and assign an owner.

sections.heading: Power-interest grid
sections.body: The power-interest grid plots stakeholders by their power to affect the project and their interest in it. Each quadrant implies a different engagement strategy. Close relatives swap one axis: the Power/Influence grid uses active involvement instead of interest, and the Influence/Impact grid uses ability to change the plan.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 205' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='44' y1='16' x2='44' y2='172' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='44' y1='172' x2='306' y2='172' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='175' y1='16' x2='175' y2='172' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8' stroke-dasharray='4 4'/><line x1='44' y1='94' x2='306' y2='94' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8' stroke-dasharray='4 4'/><text x='109' y='58' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Keep Satisfied</text><text x='240' y='58' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Manage Closely</text><text x='109' y='136' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Monitor</text><text x='240' y='136' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Keep Informed</text><text x='26' y='94' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 26 94)'>Power</text><text x='175' y='195' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Interest</text></svg>
figure.caption: Power up the side, interest across the bottom; each quadrant has its own strategy.

groups.label: High power, high interest
lines: Involve them and meet their expectations.
Partner and collaborate; these drive success.

groups.label: High power, low interest
lines: Enough attention that they don't become blockers.
Don't overload them, but don't lose them.

rows.value: Authority vs concern, the default grid.
Authority vs active involvement.
Involvement vs ability to change the project.
Power, urgency and legitimacy combined.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: High power + high interest = manage closely. High power + low interest = keep satisfied. The grid feeds the engagement plan; the stakeholder register still says who they are. Re-plot as positions change.

sections.heading: Salience model
sections.body: The salience model adds a third dimension. It scores each stakeholder on power, legitimacy and urgency, then uses the overlaps of a Venn diagram to set priority. The more attributes someone holds, the more salient they are.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 227 238' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='68' cy='86.5' r='56' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.12' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.2'/><circle cx='148' cy='86.5' r='56' fill='#2F6F5E' fill-opacity='0.12' stroke='#2F6F5E' stroke-width='1.2'/><circle cx='108' cy='148.5' r='56' fill='#B5462F' fill-opacity='0.12' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.2'/><text x='68' y='22.5' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='10.5' text-anchor='middle'>Power</text><text x='148' y='22.5' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='10.5' text-anchor='middle'>Legitimacy</text><text x='108' y='222.5' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10.5' text-anchor='middle'>Urgency</text><text x='32' y='92.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='7.5' text-anchor='middle'>Dormant</text><text x='178' y='92.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='7.5' text-anchor='middle'>Discretionary</text><text x='108' y='186.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='7.5' text-anchor='middle'>Demanding</text><rect x='89' y='55.5' width='38' height='10' fill='%BG%'/><text x='108' y='63.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='7.5' text-anchor='middle'>Dominant</text><rect x='48' y='125.5' width='44' height='10' fill='%BG%'/><text x='70' y='133.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='7.5' text-anchor='middle'>Dangerous</text><rect x='124' y='125.5' width='44' height='10' fill='%BG%'/><text x='146' y='133.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='7.5' text-anchor='middle'>Dependent</text><rect x='81' y='99.5' width='54' height='12' fill='%BG%'/><text x='108' y='108.5' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>Definitive</text></svg>
figure.caption: Three attributes overlap; a stakeholder with all three (Definitive) is highest priority.

rows.value: Can they influence the outcome or withhold resources?
Is their involvement proper and authorised, such as a sponsor, owner or regulator?
Is their claim time-critical, or does only the eventual outcome matter regardless of timing?

groups.label: Dormant (power only)
lines: e.g. a senior manager in another division.

groups.label: Discretionary (legitimacy only)
lines: e.g. an NGO, or a team member giving feedback.

groups.label: Demanding (urgency only)
lines: e.g. the CEO's assistant chasing information.

groups.label: Dominant (power + legitimacy)
lines: e.g. your boss, or a government clearance office.

groups.label: Dependent (legitimacy + urgency)
lines: No power, so they need a champion or coalition.
e.g. local residents affected by construction.

groups.label: Dangerous (power + urgency)
lines: No legitimacy - coercive, may bypass process.
e.g. a group threatening disruptive action.

groups.label: Definitive
lines: The centre of the diagram, your highest priority.
e.g. your executive sponsor or the key customer.

rows.value: Definitive and Dependent.
Dangerous - keep satisfied to contain the threat.
Dominant and Demanding.
Dormant and Discretionary.
Outside all three circles - no power, legitimacy or urgency, so no active engagement.

blocks.title: Urgency is volatile
blocks.text: Urgency spikes with deadlines and crises. A Discretionary or Dependent stakeholder can jump to Definitive overnight, so re-score regularly.

sections.heading: Stakeholder Cube
sections.body: The Stakeholder Cube takes the 2D Power/Interest grid and adds attitude: supportive backer versus opposed blocker. Combining power, interest and attitude gives eight stakeholder characters. Its value is that two stakeholders with identical power and interest may need opposite handling depending on whether they back or block you.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 212' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='155' x2='120' y2='155' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' opacity='0.55'/><line x1='120' y1='155' x2='120' y2='75' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' opacity='0.55'/><line x1='120' y1='75' x2='40' y2='75' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' opacity='0.55'/><line x1='40' y1='75' x2='40' figure.caption: Power x interest x attitude - eight corners, from Saviour (high power, active, backer) to Irritant.

groups.label: Saboteur
lines: The biggest threat; manage and contain.

groups.label: Sleeping giant
lines: Wake them up - a powerful supporter not yet engaged.

groups.label: Time bomb
lines: Dormant opposition that could detonate; watch closely.

groups.label: Trip wire
lines: Minor but vocal opposition; can trip you up.

blocks.title: What to remember
blocks.text: The cube is the most detailed but least commonly tested. Know that it adds attitude, backer vs blocker, to power and interest, and that a high-power blocker, Saboteur or Time bomb, is the corner to fear.

sections.heading: Engagement assessment and model choice
sections.body: Classification tells you who matters; the stakeholder engagement-assessment matrix tracks how engaged each one is versus how engaged they need to be. Classification models feed the stakeholder register and engagement plan, and the assessment matrix shows where engagement actions are needed.

rows.value: Does not know about the project or its impact.
Aware but opposed to the work or its outcomes.
Aware but neither supportive nor resistant.
Aware and supportive of the work and outcomes.
Aware and actively engaged in ensuring success.
Fast, intuitive; good for most projects and for communicating with leadership.
When timing and legitimacy matter - regulators, crisis response, or stakeholders whose urgency swings.
When attitude is decisive and you need to separate supporters from opponents who otherwise look identical.

blocks.title: Exam clue
blocks.text: 'Power, legitimacy, and urgency' = salience model. 'Power and interest' = the grid. Adding 'attitude' or backer/blocker = the cube. Current vs desired engagement = the engagement-assessment matrix.

blocks.title: Close the gap
blocks.text: Mark C = current and D = desired for each stakeholder. Where C and D differ, plan engagement actions to move them. People move between categories as the project changes, so re-map at major milestones.

sections.heading: Quick distinctions
sections.body: Use these side-by-side cues to avoid the common exam traps.

rows.value: Pareto - focus on the vital few.
Control chart - stable process vs special cause.
Burndown - work remaining.

rows.key: Line rising to a scope line
rows.value: Burn-up - work completed vs scope.
Fishbone - possible root causes.
Kanban shows flow of work; RACI shows ownership of work.
RACI is one form of RAM; RAM is the general responsibility grid.
Stakeholder register = who they are; power-interest grid = how to engage.
Matrix = risk priority; risk register = the full list and responses.

blocks.title: Final tip
blocks.text: Match the visual to the performance domain the question is really about. Pick the tailored, value-delivering option over the heaviest process; boards, charts and matrices inform decisions, and the exam rewards acting on them.

source: PM Cram — aligned to the current PMBOK Guide and PMI Examination Content Outline.
